back: Unified command-line tool for scripting/automating interactions with any AWS service via its API — the scripting counterpart to the Management Console; exam relevance is mostly around knowing it's the tool for automation/scripting rather than any specific feature.

Infrastructure-as-Code service: define AWS resources in declarative JSON/YAML templates, then create/update/delete them as a single managed 'stack' with automatic rollback on failure.

StackSets extend this to deploy the same template across multiple accounts/regions — a key exam pattern for consistent multi-account provisioning (often alongside AWS Organizations/Control Tower).

Records API-level activity (who did what, when, from where) across the AWS account/organization for auditing, governance, and security investigation — every console, CLI, and SDK action shows up as an event here.

Central metrics, alarms, and dashboards service — collects metrics from AWS resources (and custom metrics), triggers alarms (e.g. to feed Auto Scaling or SNS), and visualizes operational health.

The core building block for automated reactive scaling and operational monitoring across the exam.

Centralized log aggregation from EC2, Lambda, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and more, with search/filter, metric filters (turn log patterns into CloudWatch metrics/alarms), and subscription filters (stream logs in real time to Lambda, Kinesis, or OpenSearch) — the log-specific half of CloudWatch, distinct from the metrics/alarms half.

Analyzes historical utilization (CPU, memory, network via CloudWatch Agent) of EC2, Auto Scaling groups, EBS, Lambda, and ECS on Fargate, then recommends more cost/performance-optimal resource types/sizes — a rightsizing recommendation engine, distinct from Trusted Advisor's broader best-practice checks.

Continuously records AWS resource configurations and evaluates them against rules (managed or custom) for compliance, keeping a full configuration history/timeline per resource.

Exam-critical: Config answers 'is this resource compliant with our policy, and what did its configuration look like at any point in time' — paired with CloudTrail (who made the API call) and often triggering remediation via Systems Manager Automation or Lambda.

Automates setting up a secure, well-architected multi-account AWS environment (a 'landing zone') on top of AWS Organizations, applying preventive (SCP-based) and detective (Config-based) guardrails automatically.

Exam trigger: 'quickly and consistently set up governance across many new/existing AWS accounts' — Control Tower orchestrates Organizations + Config + SSO/IAM Identity Center together rather than being a standalone primitive.

Personalized view of AWS service health events and scheduled changes that specifically affect your account's resources (vs. the public Service Health Dashboard, which shows general regional/service status) — useful for proactive operational awareness of AWS-side issues impacting you.

Tracks and enforces software licensing rules (e.g. Microsoft, Oracle, SAP license terms) across EC2 and on-premises usage to avoid over-deployment/non-compliance — relevant to BYOL (bring-your-own-license) migration scenarios.

Managed Grafana for visualizing metrics/logs/traces from multiple sources (including CloudWatch and Prometheus) — chosen specifically when an organization already has Grafana dashboards/expertise and wants that experience without self-hosting Grafana.

Serverless, Prometheus-compatible metrics store for container workloads (commonly EKS/ECS), letting teams keep using PromQL and existing Prometheus-based tooling without operating Prometheus servers themselves — pairs naturally with Managed Grafana for visualization.
The web UI for AWS — mentioned on the exam mainly in contrast to CLI/SDK/CloudFormation as the manual, non-programmatic way of interacting with AWS.

Lets administrators curate a catalog of pre-approved CloudFormation-based products (VMs, databases, full stacks) that end users/teams can self-service launch, ensuring compliance/standardization without giving users broad provisioning permissions.

Exam trigger: 'let developers self-provision approved resources without direct IAM permissions to create them.'

Central place to view and request increases for AWS service limits (e.g. EC2 instance limits, VPC counts) across services, and to set CloudWatch alarms when usage approaches a quota — relevant whenever a scenario mentions hitting an AWS default limit.

Umbrella operations toolset for managing, patching, and securing a fleet of EC2 instances — and on-premises servers, via the SSM Agent — at scale.

A frequent source of 'operate/secure a fleet of instances without X' exam answers; see individual cards for its component tools (Session Manager, Patch Manager, Run Command, Parameter Store, State Manager).

front: Systems Manager: what does an instance need before it can be managed?
back:
The SSM Agent must be installed and running on the instance (pre-installed on most AWS-provided AMIs, such as Amazon Linux 2 and recent Windows Server), and the instance needs an IAM role/instance profile attached granting Systems Manager permissions (e.g. the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy).

Missing either is the most common reason an instance doesn't appear as 'Managed' in Systems Manager, or Session Manager 'doesn't work.'

Provides interactive shell access to an instance without SSH keys, an open inbound port, or a bastion host — the connection is initiated outbound from the instance to Systems Manager, so no inbound security group rule is needed at all.

A very common exam answer for 'securely access an instance with no bastion host and no open SSH port.'
Automates applying OS and software patches across a fleet of managed instances on a defined schedule (patch baselines plus maintenance windows), instead of patching each instance manually.

Executes ad hoc commands or scripts across many instances at once (or on a schedule), without needing to connect into each one individually — useful for fleet-wide one-off operational tasks like restarting a service or checking disk usage.

Hierarchical, centralized storage for configuration data and secrets, with three value types: String, StringList, or SecureString, where SecureString encrypts the value via KMS.

It's free/cheap and has no built-in rotation, unlike Secrets Manager — 'needs automatic rotation' is the signal to use Secrets Manager instead.

Enforces and maintains a defined configuration state on managed instances on a recurring schedule — e.g. ensuring a required agent stays installed and running, or a specific configuration file stays in place — automatically remediating drift rather than just detecting it.

Automated best-practice checks across cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, service limits, and operational excellence, with the depth of checks gated by support plan (Business/Enterprise support unlocks the full check set).

Free, self-service tool to review a workload against the six pillars of the Well-Architected Framework (operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, sustainability), producing a report of risks and improvement suggestions — a structured, workload-level architectural review, distinct from Trusted Advisor's account-wide automated checks.

front: CloudTrail vs. AWS Config — what's the difference?
back:
CloudTrail tells you WHO made a change and WHEN (the API call).

AWS Config tells you WHAT the resource configuration looked like before/after and whether it's compliant.

They're frequently paired — confusing which answers 'who changed this' vs. 'is this compliant' is a common exam trap.

front: Trusted Advisor vs. Compute Optimizer — what's the difference?
back:
Trusted Advisor runs broad best-practice/config checks across many categories.

Compute Optimizer is specifically a deep rightsizing recommendation engine driven by resource utilization data.

front: CloudTrail vs. CloudWatch — what's the difference?
back:
CloudTrail records API-level activity — every console, CLI, and SDK call made against your account, answering 'who did what, and when' for auditing and security investigation.

CloudWatch is operational monitoring — it collects metrics (CPU, latency, custom app metrics), aggregates logs, and triggers alarms that can drive Auto Scaling or notifications, answering 'how is my system performing right now.' They're complementary, not competing: CloudTrail can even deliver its event log into CloudWatch Logs, letting you alarm on specific API activity (e.g. a root login) using CloudWatch's alerting on top of CloudTrail's audit data.
